greeting: Ask in plain English. I'll think through a real answer, grounded on KnoSky docs (install, package, privacy, swarm, packs).
fallback:
I only answer from the published KnoSky FAQ. Try asking:
• How do I install?
• What is KnoSky?
• What’s in the package?
• Is my code uploaded?
• What is a swarm / L3?
• Which tools work?

Docs: https://knosky.wiki/
Install: https://www.knosky.com/install.html

entries.title: How to install
entries.simple:
1. Install **Node.js 20 or newer** on your computer (if you don’t have it).
2. Open a terminal in **your project folder**.
3. Run:

`npx knosky@latest.`

KnoSky builds a **map of your files** on your machine (and can open a **city view** of that map). No KnoSky account. Your code stays local by default.

Optional next step: connect one AI tool you already use (Claude, Cursor, Codex, or Hermes) so it can follow the map.
entries.technical: That command runs the published `knosky` package. It indexes the folder into local files under `.knosky/` (including city data) and can print connection snippets for assistant tools. After install, `npx knosky@latest doctor` is a health check for what this machine can honestly claim. Guided steps: install page and wiki “start here.”

entries.title: What’s in the package
entries.simple:
With KnoSky you get:

1. **A map of your project** on your computer
2. **A city picture** of that map for people to browse
3. **Help for AI tools** so they find the right files instead of guessing
4. **Optional yes/no checks** when you want stricter control
5. **Hooks for popular AI tools** (Claude, Cursor, Codex, Hermes)
6. **A health check** so you know what is working
7. **Early support for several AIs working together** (still unfinished — see “What is a swarm?”)
8. **Optional pull-request tips** that suggest where to look (they don’t block builds by default)

**Not included:** a coding AI that writes all your code, a KnoSky cloud vault of your source, or a promise that multi-AI work is “finished and risk-free everywhere.”
entries.technical: Product surfaces behind that list: local indexer + city HTML, MCP server (map tools + optional governed route tools), Mode A tips vs Mode B checked allow/deny, assistant packs, `doctor` health ceiling, early multi-agent coordinator (L3 foundation), PR tip Action, and docs/wiki. Explore the home “What’s in the box” section for the marketing view.

entries.title: What is KnoSky?
entries.simple:
KnoSky is **navigation for AI helpers** in large projects.

Your coding tools are good at writing. They still get **lost** in thousands of files and docs. KnoSky builds a **map** on your machine and helps them take a **better path to the right place**. People can look at the same map as a **city view**.

Think **GPS for work folders**, not “another writing brain.”
entries.technical: Category: agent GPS / route guidance. Not an IDE and not a full code-search product. Optional stricter mode adds checked identity and allow/deny style outcomes. Marketing claims should stay within what the local health check (`doctor`) shows for that install.

entries.title: What it is not
entries.simple:
KnoSky is **not** Claude, Cursor, or any coding model.

It does **not** replace the AI that writes code. It helps those AIs **find their way** in your project. In the normal setup it is also **not** “send all my code to KnoSky’s cloud.”
entries.technical: Complements coding tools; does not try to be Sourcegraph-style code intelligence or a default multi-tenant source vault. Compare page states the boundary clearly.

entries.title: Mode A vs Mode B
entries.simple:
Two levels of caution:

- **Mode A** — friendly **suggestions** only (“try looking here”). Not a formal yes/no.
- **Mode B** — **checked** answers. The system knows which assistant is asking, applies rules, and returns a clear **yes** or **no** (with a receipt when yes).

Use Mode A to explore. Use Mode B when you care about clearer control.
entries.technical: Mode A is labeled advisory-only on the wire. Mode B uses registered assistant identity (a temporary pass called a lease), policy checks, and an audit trail on allow. Day-to-day setup: install map, connect a pack, then register an assistant when you want Mode B paths. Wiki “Mode A vs B” has the steps.

entries.title: Which tools work?
entries.simple:
KnoSky is meant to ride along with tools many teams already use:

- Claude Code
- Cursor
- Codex
- Hermes

You install KnoSky, build the map, then connect your tool once so it can ask for directions. Lighter options exist too (for example PR tips).
entries.technical: Connection is usually through each tool’s MCP / connector settings: point at the local KnoSky server and the city/map file path. Pack pages on the wiki have copy-paste style setup for each host.

entries.title: Is my code uploaded?
entries.simple:
**Usually no.** KnoSky is designed to run on **your computer**. The normal path does **not** upload your source code to KnoSky as an online storage product.

If you later share a map or city file with a teammate, treat it like sharing a table of contents with short snippets — glance at it first.
entries.technical: Default core path is local-first without always-on product analytics. Optional share packaging can refuse to build when it spots secret-looking text. The marketing website is not a runtime pipeline for your repo. Health check (`doctor`) also surfaces platform limits (for example some Windows network lock features are still incomplete).

entries.title: What is the city map?
entries.simple:
The **city** is a simple picture of your project so **people** can browse it — areas as neighborhoods, files as buildings.

AI tools care more about **directions to the right file**. The city is the friendly face of the same map. Build it from **your** project with install — not a fake sample skyline on worldview marketing alone.
entries.technical: City HTML is generated from the local index. Product story leads with GPS/routing for agents; city is intentionally the human skin band. Command reminder: `npx knosky@latest.` in your repo.

entries.title: What is a swarm? (L3)
entries.simple:
**First: what “swarm” means here**

A **swarm** is just **more than one AI helper working on the same project at the same time** — like several assistants editing, searching, or reviewing together. It is **not** insects, and it is **not** “AI takes over the company by itself.”

**What L3 is**

**L3** is KnoSky’s **early traffic helper** for that situation: when several AI helpers share one project, try to **reduce collisions** — for example two of them slamming the same file at once — and keep a simple board of who is doing what.

**What L3 is not**

- Not a finished “always safe multi-AI factory for every business” product
- Not something you must turn on to get basic mapping and directions
- Not a promise that nothing will ever go wrong

If you only want KnoSky to **map your project** and **guide one assistant to the right files**, you can ignore L3 for now.
entries.technical:
**If you want more detail (still in plain language):**

L3 is Stage 3 on KnoSky’s levels of promise. Earlier stages are: local map (L0), safer sharing of map packaging (L1), and checked yes/no routes for a known assistant (L2).

The early L3 pieces on your machine can include:
- temporary passes so each assistant is a known worker
- “I’m working on this file” holds so others wait or pick another task
- simple rate limits so one helper can’t spam the system
- a status board / activity picture for operators
- small practice runs to see conflict behavior

**Honesty:** those pieces exist as an **early foundation**. The product will not claim “production multi-AI is fully safe everywhere” from this alone. A local health check (`doctor`) is the nicer way to see what *this install* actually supports.

**Ops tip without jargon:** if you run multi-agent practice tests, use a **throwaway project folder**, not your real working map, so test noise doesn’t clutter day-to-day status.

**Security note in ordinary words:** a project admin pass can still cancel a worker’s temporary pass on its own today — treat admin access carefully. Fuller dual-control rules for that may come later. Written up in SECURITY on GitHub if you need the engineer version.

entries.title: What is doctor?
entries.simple:
`doctor` is a **health check**. It tells you what looks ready on your machine and where you should stay modest in public claims.

Run:

`npx knosky@latest doctor`
entries.technical: It reports items like map/Mode B wiring, packs, levels of promise notes, multi-agent early features presence, and some platform caveats. Marketing language should not promise higher than this check for that install.

entries.title: Does it actually help?
entries.simple:
In **KnoSky’s own small test** (5 tasks), a guided path used about **68% fewer tokens**, **70% fewer tool calls**, and got to the right file about **6× faster** than wandering around.

That’s a useful signal from one test pack — not a guarantee for every project forever.
entries.technical: The study compared a guided path vs a naive wander on five tasks. Method notes live in the repo/wiki. Keep it separate from other companies’ blog numbers about agent cost — those show industry pain, not KnoSky’s scorecard.

entries.title: What can the assistant ask KnoSky?
entries.simple:
Once connected, your AI can basically ask KnoSky:

- **Find** something on the map
- **Take me** to the right place
- **Is this allowed?** (when stricter mode is on)

You don’t need the internal tool names for normal use.
entries.technical: Implementers see a fixed menu split into map lookups vs checked route tools, plus optional audit tools on a security profile. Decision outcomes use a small closed vocabulary (allow, deny reasons, advisory-only, errors). Protocol wiki lists names if you are wiring a host.

entries.title: Is it free?
entries.simple:
Yes for the core open project. Run it locally with `npx knosky@latest`. No account wall on the default path.

The software license is **FSL-1.1-MIT**: free to use, with rules against simply turning it into a competing hosted clone; it moves toward MIT later.
entries.technical: Full license text is LICENSE.md on GitHub. npm package name: `knosky` (current line 0.7.x).

entries.title: Where are the docs?
entries.simple:
- **Product story:** knosky.com
- **How-to guides:** knosky.wiki
- **Code & deep notes:** github.com/SathiaAI/knosky

Hands-on? Start at **Install**. Prefer a short guided path? Use wiki **Start here**.
entries.technical: Marketing multipage site + wiki adoption kit (packs, protocol, ladder, trust) + llms.txt for agents.
